reasoning: Portugal has very low levels of 'Violence against civilians' according to ACLED, with historic data indicating around 4 events per year (translating to a 360-day multiplier base around 1.33). For the criteria to resolve YES, we would need over 13 events (at least 14) in a single 30-day window leading up to the resolution date. This translates to an unprecedented 40-fold localized increase in political violence, which only happens during severe conflict situations (like a widespread insurgency or massive terrorist campaign). While there have been sporadic political tensions in Portugal (anti-police protests in late 2024 and the dismantling of a small far-right militia in June 2025), these do not fall into the ACLED category of 'Violence against civilians' in anywhere near the volume needed. Since there is no ongoing insurgency or terror campaign, the probability of hitting this threshold in any 30-day window is virtually zero. The probabilities are set to the minimum allowed bound (0.05) and increase slightly with horizon to appropriately account for unknown future events.
